summary: DHH distinguishes between fixed-cost businesses (like designer goods) and variable-cost businesses (like consumer electronics), arguing that fixed-cost businesses should offer better customer service since their margins are higher. He's furious about being charged $100 to replace a simple watch strap on his Jacob Jensen designer watch. He argues that when you're buying into the idea of design, you shouldn't be gouged on repairs the way you'd expect from commodity electronics.
key_points: Fixed-cost businesses have high margins and can afford generous customer service; variable-cost businesses cannot
Design-oriented luxury brands should treat repairs as part of the brand experience, not a profit center
Being charged $100 for a simple silicone strap replacement feels like a betrayal of the design-buying contract
money_quotes: When you run a variable cost business, you can't afford to be lavish with customer service or repairs. When you run a fixed cost business, you can.
I expect to be fucked on computer repairs or printer refills, but not when buying the idea of design.
key_insight: Luxury and design brands that charge premium prices for the idea of design betray their customers when they also gouge them on basic repairs.
